coredata.dc:title: pH/Reduction dual-responsive comet-shaped PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate prodrug: Synthesis and self-assembly as tumor nanotheranostics
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guoping
Pei, Mingliang
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) show promising potential for tumor imaging owing to their unique superior fluorescent properties. However, the small particle size limits their practical application. Here, pH/reduction dual-responsive comet-shaped PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate prodrug, DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG with DOX content of 28.5%, was designed with the hydrophobic acid-labile DOX conjugated CQDs as comet nucleus and the few hydrophilic bioreducible detachable PEG brushes as comet tails. The comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug could self-assemble into unique micelles with mean diameter of 127 nm. The DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles possessed excellent pH/reduction dual-responsive drug release with low drug leakage of 9% in 150 h. Furthermore, the fluorescent CQDs was recovered after DOX release and de-PEGylation, demonstrating their potential application for real-time response of therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor nanotheranostics
Comet-shaped
PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate
Self-assembly
pH/reduction dual-responsive
Fluorescence “off-on”

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and self-assembly of comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug
In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular uptake
Synthesis and self-assembly of comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug
In vitro drug release from DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles
In vitro cellular toxicity and uptake of DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical hydrodynamic diameter distribution of the CQDs (a), CQD-SS-PEG (b) and DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles in water (c), DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG in DMSO (d), and DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles after treating with pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH for 150 h (e).
TEM images of the CQDs (a), the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG (b) and the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles (c).
(A) FT-IR spectra of the CQDs (a), CQD-SS-PEG (b), Hy-CQD-SS-PEG (c) and DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG (d), (B) UV–vis spectra of DOX, CQDs, Hy-CQD-SS-PEG and DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles in aqueous media.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CQDs (0.01 mg/mL), (b) Hy-CQD-SS-PEG (0.20 mg/mL) and (c) Hy-CQD-SS-PEG-DOX micelles (0.20 mg/mL) under different wavelength excitation from 340 to 410 nm. Inset: digital images of CQDs (0.20 mg/mL), Hy-CQD-SS-PEG (0.20 mg/mL) and Hy-CQD-SS-PEG-DOX micelles (0.20 mg/mL) in aqueous media under daylight (left) and ultraviolet light (right) at 365 nm.

(A) In vitro DOX release profiles DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles in different releasing media, (B) In vitro cell viability of HepG2 cells incubated with different concentrations of free DOX, Hy-CQD-SS-PEG, and DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles for 24 h and (C) incubated with 40 μg/mL of free DOX, Hy-CQD-SS-PEG, or DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles for 48 h. Data present mean ± SD (n = 3).
Fluorescent images of HepG2 cells stained by DAPI and incubated with DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles for 24 h.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the pH/reduction dual-responsive comet-shaped PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate prodrug.
Fitted DOX release parameters with Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models.

head.ce:title: pH/Reduction dual-responsive comet-shaped PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate prodrug: Synthesis and self-assembly as tumor nanotheranostics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) show promising potential for tumor imaging owing to their unique superior fluorescent properties. However, the small particle size limits their practical application. Here, pH/reduction dual-responsive comet-shaped PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate prodrug, DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG with DOX content of 28.5%, was designed with the hydrophobic acid-labile DOX conjugated CQDs as comet nucleus and the few hydrophilic bioreducible detachable PEG brushes as comet tails. The comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug could self-assemble into unique micelles with mean diameter of 127 nm. The DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles possessed excellent pH/reduction dual-responsive drug release with low drug leakage of 9% in 150 h. Furthermore, the fluorescent CQDs was recovered after DOX release and de-PEGylation, demonstrating their potential application for real-time response of therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, have attracted more and more interests for biomedical applications in the last decade, due to their advantages of high stability, unique optical properties, good biocompatibility, great aqueous stability, low photobleaching and cost, simple synthetic, etc. [1]. They are composed of a sp2-carbon core and multiple oxygen-containing surface (i.e. carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aldehyde groups), which endows facile surface functionalization for various applications [2].

The desired fluorescent property and good biocompatibility make CQDs potential candidate for biomedical imaging application, or tumor theranostics as all-in-one platform with both diagnosis and chemotherapy after drug-loading [3]. However, as a fluorescent imaging agent or drug carrier, the circulation lifetime of CQDs in blood suffers from the rapid urinary excretion via renal clearance, because of their small diameter [4]. To solve this problem, various strategies have been developed to fabricate the CQDs-based nanoplatforms with desired diameter for biomedical applications, as crosslinker in nanogels [5–7], building block for hybrid nanosponges [8,9], or nanocomposites [10–14].

Recently, CQD-based nanoassemblies have been reported for tumor nanotheranostics. The nanotheranostics could be easily fabricated by non-covalent drug-loading onto the CQD-based nanoassemblies, which were obtained by the self-assembly of CQDs after modification with cationic surfactants [15,16]. Our group has reported the nanoassemblies of the Janus-like PMMA-PEG-FA grafted CQDs for leakage-free tumor theranostic application, via SI-ATRP of methyl methacrylate (MMA) in ethanol from the CQD-based macro-initiator, followed by the PEGylation via click chemistry with folic acid (FA) mon-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG-FA) [17]. After loading DOX with loading capacity of 67.2%, the DOX-loaded nanoassemblies showed excellent tumor intracellular on-demand drug release, with cumulative release of >80% at pH 5.0 within 36 h, while only 2% at pH 7.4. Park et al. designed pluronic mimicking fluorescent carbon nanoparticles conjugated with DOX via acid-cleavable linkage for tumor-targeted drug delivery and bioimaging [18]. However, a low drug content and high drug leakage of 40% in normal pH media were obtained, because of the loose nanoassemblies due to the outer PEO-rich shell.

In the present work, PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate prodrug (DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG) was designed by conjugating DOX via acid-labile hydrazone linkage onto the few PEG brushes modified CQDs via bioreducible disulfide bond (Scheme 1 ). Owing to the hydrophobic DOX conjugated CQDs and the few hydrophilic PEG brushes, comet-shaped conjugate prodrug was obtained with DOX content of 28.5%, and could self-assemble into unique micelles with mean diameter of 127 nm. The DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles possessed excellent pH/reduction dual-responsive drug release with low drug leakage of 9% in 150 h. After DOX release and de-PEGylation in the simulated tumor intracellular media, high fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was recovered, demonstrating their potential application for real-time response of chemotherapy.

PEG-COOH (Mn = 2 kDa) was purchased from Shanghai Jinpan Biotech Co., Ltd. Triethylamine (TEA) and dichloromethane (DCM) were obtained from Lanzhou Gongchuang Technology E-Commerce Co., Ltd. Cystamine dihydrochloride (Cy, 98%) was purchased from J&K Chemical Co., Ltd. Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) was purchased from Shanghai Kefeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium citrate (99%, AR) and NH4HCO3 (20.0–21.0% NH3 by weight, AR) were obtained from Tianjin chemical reagent company. 1-(3-Dimethylaminoproyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl, 98%) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, ˃80.0%) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl, 99.4%) was provided by Beijing Huafang United Technology Co., Ltd. Double distilled water was used throughout.

The CQDs were synthesized according to the hydrothermal method reported previously [8]. Briefly, a solution of sodium citrate (1.40 g) and NH4HCO3 (10.5 g) in 70 mL of water was sealed in an autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the clear brownish yellow solution was dialyzed (MW = 1000 Da) against water for 3 days, replacing water each 8 h. Finally, the CQD solution with solid content of 8.0 mg/mL was stored for use. The relative molecular weight of the as-prepared CQDs was measured as 769 Da, with a 6120 Quadrupole LC/MS (Agilent Technologies).

PEG-SS-NH2 was synthesized by the amidation of the carboxyl of PEG-COOH with Cy. Generally, 0.5 g of PEG-COOH (0.25 mmol), 0.15 g of DCC (0.73 mmol) and 0.12 g of NHS (1.04 mmol) were dissolved into 10 mL of DCM with stirring for 2 h at room temperature to active carboxyl groups. Subsequently, a mixture solution containing 278 μL of TEA and 0.11 g of cystamine dihydrochloride (0.49 mmol) in 24 mL of DMSO/DCM (vol. ratio of 1/5) was added dropwise into PEG-COOH solution and the final solution was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. After filtration, concentration, the PEG-SS-NH2 was obtained as white powder after precipitation in ice ethyl alcohol and drying in vacuum drying oven at 45 °C, with a yield of 64.19% (1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, δ, ppm): a (3.36 CH 3O-), b (3.63 -OCH 2CH 2O-), c (4.24 -CH 2CONH-), d (2.62 -CH 2SSCH
2 CH2NH2), e (2.81 -CH2CONHCH2 -) (Fig. S1)).

The PEGylated CQDs were prepared by the amidation between the carboxyl groups in CQDs and the amino terminated group in the GSH-responsive PEG-SS-NH2: 5 mL of CQDs solution (containing of 40 mg CQDs), 1.93 mg of EDC·HCI (0.01 mmol) and 1.15 mg of NHS (0.01 mmol) were stirred at room temperature for 30 min, then, 2 mg of PEG-SS-NH2 was added, with a molar ratio between CQDs and PEG-SS-NH2 of 55.5. The mix solution was reacted with stirring in dark at room temperature for 24 h. The yellow PEGylated CQDs were obtained after dialysis (MW = 1000 Da) against water to remove off the bare CQDs and freeze drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and self-assembly of comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrazine modified PEGylated CQDs (Hy-CQD-SS-PEG) were synthesized by the amidation of the remained carboxyl groups in the PEGylated CQDs (CQD-SS-PEG) with hydrazine, as following: 40 mg of CQD-SS-PEG and 3 mL of hydrazine hydrate were mixed in 60 mL of water with stirring at room temperature for 36 h in dark. The Hy-CQD-SS-PEG was obtained after dialysis (MW = 3500 Da) against water and freeze drying.

Then the comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug was synthesized by conjugation of DOX onto the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG through acid-labile hydrazone bond. Hy-CQD-SS-PEG (50 mg) and DOX·HCI (35 mg) were dissolved into DMSO (20 mL). After TEA (350 μL) and TFA (17.5 μL) were added, the reaction was stirred in dark for 24 h. The DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles was collected through dialyzing (MW = 3500 Da) against water for 3 days and freeze drying.

The DOX content in the comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug was determined UV–vis spectroscopy at 480 nm after disintegration by treating with 1.2 mol/L HCl solution for 60 h [19] and removal of the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG by centrifugation (12,000 rpm for 10 min).
1H NMR spectra were recorded by using a Nuclear Magnetic Resonance instrument (JEOL ECS 400M) spectrometer with CDCl3 as solvent.
Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer of wavenumber (4000–400 cm− 1) with the KBr pellet technique.
The steady-state emission spectra of the CQD samples were detected in their aqueous dispersions on a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer.
The morphology and diameter of the samples were characterized using a JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscopy (TEM).
Hydrodynamic diameter and distribution of the samples were measured in aqueous media with dynamic scattered light (DLS, BI-200SM) at 21 °C.

The release behavior of DOX from the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles was assessed at pH 7.4 PBS and pH 5.0 ABS with or without GSH at 37 °C. The DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles were dispersed into 10 mL of the buffer solution with a solid content of 3 mg/mL, and the dispersion was placed in a dialysis bag (MW = 3500 Da). The dialysis bag was immersed in the corresponding releasing medium (110 mL) with sharking at 120 rpm at 37 °C. At intervals time, 5 mL of the dialysate was taken out for the DOX concentration measurement using UV–vis spectroscopy, and the same volume of corresponding fresh buffer solution was added to keep the dialysate volume constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the proposed prodrug micelles was evaluated in vitro via MTT assay on the HepG2 cell lines. The cells were cultivated on 96-well plates (1.0 × 105 cells per well) in 100 μL of DMEM containing 10% FBS, and incubated in atmospheric humidity (5% CO2, 95% air, 37 °C) for 24 h. Then, the prodrug micelles or free DOX were added at different concentrations. After further incubation for 24 h, 20.0 μL of MTT (5.0 mg/mL) was added into each well, followed with further incubation for 4 h. Then the MTT-containing medium were removed and replaced with 100 μL of DMSO. After the crystal substances were dissolved for 20 min in each well, the absorbance of the solution was measured at 490 nm on a microplate reader.

The in vitro cellular uptake of the proposed prodrug micelles was exhibited on a confocal fluorescence microscope (CLSM, DMI 4000B, LEICA, Germany) using HepG2 cells after 24 h incubation, with a concentration of 15 μg/mL. Then, the cells were washed with the culture solution, fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and dyed with DAPI. The fluorescence was analyzed at the excitation wavelengths of 480 nm (DOX) and 461 nm (DAPI).

The CQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method with sodium citrate and ammonium bicarbonate as raw materials. They were spherical shape and well-dispersed without aggregation. The mean hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) and average particle size were determined as 3.6 ± 0.2 nm and 2.1 ± 0.1 nm from the DLS (Fig. 1a) and TEM (Fig. 2a) analysis, respectively. In the FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3A), the broad absorbance around 3350 cm− 1 is assigned to the NH and OH stretching vibration, while the peaks at 1645 and 1415 cm− 1 are attributed to the CO and the CC (sp2 carbon)/amide CN stretching vibration, respectively [20]. Furthermore, the signals at 2924 and 1157 cm− 1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of -CH3 and C-O/C-N-C in amine, respectively [21]. The results demonstrated the as-synthesized CQDs were graphitized carbon with surface hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and methyl groups.

In the UV–vis analysis (Fig. 3B), the two adsorption peaks of the CQDs at 235 nm and 335 nm are attributed to the π → π* transitions of the aromatic CC bond in the core and the n → π* transitions of the CO bond and amino groups on the surface, respectively [20,21]. The latter one would result in strong fluorescence. The aqueous dispersion of the CQDs (0.20 mg/mL) exhibited very strong bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light at 365 nm (insert in Fig. 4a). The fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs were recorded at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4a). Increasing the excitation wavelength from 340 to 410 nm, the fluorescence intensity decreased distinctly, while the emission peaks centered at 445 nm in all cases. The results indicated the excitation dependent fluorescence emission feature of the as-synthesized CQDs [21].

The PEG-SS-NH2 was synthesized to PEGylate the CQDs via bioreducible disulfide linkage. The integral area ratio between the protons b (3.63 -OCH2CH2O- in PEG) and d (2.62 CH2SSCH2CH2NH2 in Cy) was 173:7.3 (Fig. S1), near to the theoretical ratio of 173:8.0, indicating the successful synthesis. Then the PEGylated CQDs were synthesized by grafting PEG-SS-NH2 onto their surface via amidation with a low mass feeding ratio of 5%. The characteristic absorbance peaks of the C-O-C and -CH2- of PEG and the CO of amide appeared at 2970, 1108 and 1605 cm− 1, respectively (Fig. 3A). Furthermore, the peak at 1420 cm− 1 of CN stretching vibration increased obviously. The results further revealed the successful synthesis of the CQD-SS-PEG via the amidation of the carboxyl groups in CQDs with PEG-SS-NH2. After the PEGylation, the Dh of the products increased slightly to 4.3 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 1b), demonstrating that few PEG brushes had been grafted onto the CQDs (as shown in Scheme 1).

To conjugate DOX via acid-labile bond, the remained surface carboxyl groups in the CQD-SS-PEG were then modified with excess hydrazine. The absorbance peak intensity at 1605 cm− 1 of the CO of amide stretching vibration was much higher than the others of the C-O-C, -CH3 and -CH2- in the CQD-SS-PEG, and the broad absorbance centered at 3421 cm− 1 of the NH stretching vibration increased (Fig. 3A), revealing the amidation of the CQD-SS-PEG with hydrazine. The resultant Hy-CQD-SS-PEG showed unique separated spheres with average particle size of 3.9 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 2b), indicating there was no inter-particle linkage of the CQD-SS-PEG in the amidation of with excess hydrazine.

The Hy-CQD-SS-PEG showed similar UV adsorption spectra as the bare CQDs, with a slight blue shift of the n → π* transitions of the CO bond to 332 nm (Fig. 3B). However, their fluorescence intensity was much lower than the bare CQDs (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the maximum emission intensity was achieved at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and the fluorescence emission spectra showed a red shift from 448 to 479 nm with a decrease of fluorescence intensity, with increasing the excitation wavelength from 340 to 410 nm. The red shift of emission peak should be resulted from the reduced bandgap due to the increased number of nitrogen species after amidation of with hydrazine [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and self-assembly of comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug was synthesized by conjugating DOX onto the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG via an acid-labile hydrazone linkage. The absorbance peak of CC at 1446 cm− 1 became strong after the DOX conjugation, due to the aromatic rings in DOX. And the peak appeared at 1263 cm− 1, assigned to the C-O-C group in DOX (Fig. 3A) [23]. The results demonstrated the successful DOX conjugation. Unavoidably, the surface primary amine on the CQDs could also conjugate DOX via imine bond, which could also be cleaved off in acidic media [24]. Such imine-conjugated DOX is favorable to increase the drug content, but not affect the pH triggered drug release from the proposed acid-labile prodrug.

The DOX content of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug was determined as 28.5% by the UV–vis spectrometer after disintegration with 1.2 mol/L HCl solution and removal of the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG. The DOX content was much higher than the drug loading capacity (DLC, denoted as the mass ratio of loaded DOX and carrier) of the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG after non-covalent bond, such as hydrogen bond. The DLC was only 8.91% and 4.28%, after stirring Hy-CQD-SS-PEG (4.0 mg) and DOX·HCI (2.0 mg) in 5.0 mL aqueous solution at pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 in dark for 24 h, respectively. The results indicated that the surface carboxyl groups on the CQD-SS-PEG had been modified with hydrazine. After the non-covalent drug-loading, the transparency of the system was poorer at pH 7.4 than pH 5.0. On the other hand, the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug could only self-assemble into micelles in water. The phenomena indicated the comet-shaped structure of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG conjugate prodrug, with the hydrophobic DOX conjugated CQDs as comet nucleus and the few hydrophilic PEG brushes as comet tails (Scheme 1). Such structure is easy to self-assemble into micelles with the hydrophobic comet nucleus as core and the hydrophilic comet tails as shell.

The DLS analysis technique was used to reveal the self-assembly of the comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug. The Dh was 127.5 ± 6.4 nm in water as a dispersion, but only 13.2 ± 0.7 nm in DMSO as a solution (Fig. 1c and d). Different from the loose ones self-assembled with the pluronic mimicking carbon nanoparticles conjugated with doxorubicin reported previously [18], the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles exhibited compact cores with average particle size of 71.4 ± 3.6 nm in the TEM analysis (Fig. 2c), because of the high conjugation degree of DOX and the low PEGylation degree in the proposed comet-shaped DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug. The micelles with compact core are expected to inhibit the drug leakage, owing to their stable structure.

The absorption at 335 nm became very weak while strong adsorption of DOX appeared in the UV–vis spectrum of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles (Fig. 3B). As a result, the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles showed weaker fluorescence intensity than the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG, although the similar emission peak and red shift with increasing the excitation wavelength (Fig. 4c). The results should be resulted from the fluorescent quenching of the CQDs after DOX conjugation [25] and the aggregation-caused quenching after self-assembly [26] via the FRET process. Based on the fluorescence analysis results, the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles could emit weak fluorescence, while a high fluorescence intensity would be recovered after the drug release. Such feature makes the proposed DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles promising “off-on” tumor nanotheranostics with the drug release as switch: weak fluorescence in the normal physiological medium as the “off” state while strong fluorescence after pH-triggered DOX release in the tumor intracellular microenvironment as the “on” state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release from DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles
ce:section.ce:para: The pH/reduction dual-triggered release performance of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles were evaluated under different pH values, with or without GSH. As shown in Fig. 5A, the drug leakage at pH 7.4 PBS was only 8.0%, 9.0% in the simulated normal physiological medium (pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH) in 150 h. The leakage was much lower than the reported loose nanoassemblies of 40%, owing to the compact structure of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles (Fig. 3c). In the acidic media, the drug release was much faster than in the neutral media, in a sustained release mode without burst release. The hydrazone conjugation could be cleaved off in the acidic media (pH 5.0 ABS) [27], releasing DOX. In the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 ABS + 10 mM GSH), the surface PEG brushes could be detached responding the high reductant level, favoring the diffusion of the released DOX out of the micelles [28]. The cumulative drug release ratio reached equilibrium in 6 days, as 86.7%. The remained drug which was not release should be due to the non-covalent adsorption of the released DOX onto the CQDs after disintegration. Calculated from the non-covalent DLC of the Hy-CQD-SS-PEG at pH 5.0 of 4.28%, about one ninth of the released DOX could be adsorbed onto the CQDs after disintegration. Taking account of the non-covalent adsorption, the conjugated DOX could be completely cleaved off from the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG.

To reveal the disintegration, the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles were treated with pH 5.0 ABS + 10 mM GSH for 150 h. Then the dispersion was characterized with DLS. Only narrow hydrodynamic distribution around 5.3 ± 0.3 nm could be seen (Fig. 1e). The Dh was slightly bigger than the bare CQDs in water, due to the surface modification of the CQDs with hydrazine. The results demonstrated the absolute disintegration of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG micelles after drug release and PEG detachment.

The drug release profiles were fitted with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models to investigate the release mechanism (Fig. S2). The fitted data, including the regression coefficient (R2), kinetic constant (k) and release exponent (n), are summarized in Table 1 . All the regression coefficients in the Higuchi model were much higher than those in the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, indicating the drug release mechanism could be better described with the Higuchi kinetic model of the Fickian diffusion mechanism [29]. The kinetic constant (k) increased with increasing the acidity or GSH concentration in the releasing media, also demonstrating the pH/reduction dual-triggered drug release performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular toxicity and uptake of DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular toxicity of the proposed DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles was then assessed in vitro with the MTT assay, in comparison with the bare Hy-CQD-SS-PEG and free DOX after incubation for 24 h with different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5B, the bare Hy-CQD-SS-PEG gave a cell viability of >81% at a high concentration of 20 μg/mL. And the cell viability was around 71% even after incubation with 40 μg/mL of Hy-CQD-SS-PEG for 48 h (Fig. 5C), demonstrating good cytocompatibility.

The DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles showed a concentration dependent cytotoxicity similar as the free DOX, although the lower apparent cytotoxicity than the free DOX for all dosages. Taking account of the DOX content of 28.5% and the cumulative release ratio of 25.0% in 24 h of incubation, the actual cumulative DOX concentration was only 1.13 μg/mL with a dose of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles at 20 μg/mL. However, the cell viability was 51.0% at such case, near the value of free DOX at 10 μg/mL (50.5%), indicating the enhanced antitumor efficacy of the proposed DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles than the free DOX [30]. Further increasing the dosage and incubation time, such as 40 μg/mL for 48 h, the cell viability after incubation with DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles (17.98%) was more near to that with free DOX (14.37%) (Fig. 5C), although the DOX content of 28.5% and cumulative release ratio of 47% in 48 h. The enhanced tumor cell growth inhibition should be resulted from the different cellular internalization approaches: free DOX via passive diffusion while the prodrug micelles via the endocytosis, which could minimize the P-glycoprotein (P-gp)-mediated drug efflux [19].

The cellular uptake and intracellular biodistribution of the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles were assessed by the CLSM technique, with HepG2 cells after incubation for 24 h (Fig. 6 ). The cell nuclei were stained with DAPI, showing blue fluorescence. DOX and CQDs showed red and green fluorescence in the cytoplasm, respectively. In the merged images, the blue cell nuclei were overlaid with the red fluorescence, meaning the DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG prodrug micelles had been internalized into the HepG2 cells, in where DOX was then released and partially accumulated in the cell nuclei [17]. The red fluorescence in the cytoplasm was resulted from the sustained drug release.

Furthermore, it is clearly that the red and green fluorescent regions overlapped completely with each other, and both overlaid the blue cell nuclei. The results demonstrated the same intracellular biodistribution of the released DOX and the disintegrated CQDs, in both the cytoplasm and the cell nuclei, similar as the released DOX. The strong fluorescence of the disintegrated CQDs could be used for the real-time imaging during the tumor chemotherapy to track the chemotherapeutic effect [11].

In summary, pH/reduction dual-responsive comet-shaped PEGylated CQD-DOX conjugate prodrug, DOX-Hy-CQD-SS-PEG with DOX content of 28.5%, was designed the hydrophobic DOX conjugated CQDs as comet nucleus and the few hydrophilic detachable PEG brushes as comet tails, by conjugating DOX via acid-labile bond and grafting PEG via bioreducible disulfide linkage. The unique micelles with mean diameter of 127 nm could be easily obtained by the self-assembly of the proposed comet-shaped prodrug. The resultant prodrug micelles possessed excellent pH/reduction dual-responsive drug release performance, releasing all conjugated DOX in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment but a low drug leakage of 9% in the simulated normal physiological medium in 150 h. The MTT assays revealed the enhanced tumor cell growth inhibition than the free DOX. Furthermore, the tumor intracellular drug release could be acted as the switch for the fluorescence “off-on” of the proposed tumor nanotheranostics, indicating the promising potential in on-demand drug delivery and real-time imaging during the tumor chemotherapy to track the chemotherapeutic effect.
Guoping Li: Investigation, Data curation. Mingliang Pei: Investigation. Peng Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.110653.
